content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
· 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
f (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
I 
John L. Fellows III 
City Attorney 
City of Torrance 
3031 Torrance Blvd. 
Torrance, CA 90503-5059 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-16-226 
Dear Mr. Fellows: 
December 19, 2016 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Torrance City Councilmember 
Mike Griffiths regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section 1090. 
QUESTIONS 
1. For the purpose of applying Regulation 18702.2, may Councilmember Griffiths measure 
from the boundary of his residence to the boundary of affected parcels as opposed to the boundary 
of the entire Torrance Municipal Airport ("Airport") where the parcel is located? 
2. May Councilmember Griffiths participate in a City Council decision regarding an appeal 
of a Planning Commission decision to approve modification of South Bay Lexus' Conditional Use 
Permit? 
CONCLUSIONS 
1. So long as the decision applies to specific boundaries or discrete areas within the Airport 
and it is not interlinked with other decisions involving the entire Airport parcel, the Councilmember 
may measure from the project boundaries. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-16-226 
Page No. 2 
2. Yes. The Councilmember does not have a disqualifying conflict of interest in the decision 
in question. However, if the facts should change regarding the appeal and related decisions you 
should seek additional advice. 
FACTS 
The City of Torrance is governed by a seven-member city council. Council member Griffiths 
personal residence is located on Cricklewood Street in Torrance, CA 90503. The Councilmember's 
property is within 500 feet of the southern portion of the Airport parcel and Parcel 52, the South 
Bay Lexus dealership ("dealership") on 3215 Pacific Coast Highway ("PCH"). 
The Airport consists of one large legal parcel, Tract No. 9765, on the County Assessor's 
Map of the same parcel. The Airport parcel runs from Skypark Drive to Pacific Coast Highway and 
then from Madison Street to a little past Crenshaw Boulevard where Rolling Hills Plaza is located. 
The City has filed Official Map No.2, creating smaller parcels for leasing purposes and for 
financing commercial businesses on parts of the airport legal parcel that are not used for airport 
operations. The map creating the smaller parcels was recorded in 1996. 
The City is considering an appeal of a Planning Commission decision to approve 
modifications of South Bay Lexus' Conditional Use Permit on Parcel 30 of the Official Map No. 2. 
If the City Council denies the appeal, then an item to modify the South Bay Lexus lease to add 
Parcels 46 and 4 7 of Official Map 2 to the Lexus leasehold will be brought before the City Council. 
Parcels 46 and 47 are currently vacant lots and are approximately 3,300 feet away from the 
Councilmember's residence. Because the parcels are currently vacant lots, the approval of an 
amendment to the South Bay Lexus lease would increase the intensity of use and may alter traffic 
levels on Crenshaw Boulevard. 
Modification of the South Bay Lexus lease would also move operations of the dealership on 
PCH (Parcel 52) to Parcels 46 and 47. 
The dealership on PCH consists of a showroom, inventory parking areas, parking and some 
service areas that have been converted to inventory, detail, and vehicle purchase areas. If the 
dealership moves, it is possible that this structure and leasehold may be used or developed for some 
other purpose. However, it will most likely be re-occupied by another automobile dealership, with 
or without vehicle service operations. An analysis by the City's Community Development 
Department indicates that regardless of "service amenities" offered by another dealership, "there 
should be no substantial change of use as any area dedicated to service amenities would result in 
correlating reduction of inventory and sales areas ... " 
Additionally, prior to issuance of building permits for another operator (dealership or other 
use), all relevant requirements must be met and permits approved to ensure that noise, air quality, 
parking, health and safety impacts would be mitigated and would meet City ordinances and 
standards. 


File No. A-16-226 
Page No. 3 
You do not believe that there will be issues with view, privacy, noise levels, or air quality 
because of the distance between Councilmember Griffith's residence and the subject properties. 
You also do not believe the market value of the Councilmember's property on Cricklewood Street 
would be affected because of its distance from Parcels 46 and 47, and because of the buffer area 
between the residence and the dealership on Parcel 52. 
The dealership is buffered from residential uses to the south by PCH, a I 00-foot wide state 
highway, the Torrance Municipal Airport local street (Airport Drive) to the North, and various 
automobile dealerships to the West and East. 
The City's Community Development Department analyzed the issue and opined, "that there 
will not be any substantial alteration of traffic levels, intensity of use, including parking, or property 
surrounding the official's real property parcel, the view, privacy, noise levels, or air quality, 
including odors, or any other factors that would affect the market value or financial interests of 
Councilman Griffith's real property." 
In addition to the upcoming leasing issue relating to South Bay Lexus, the City Council 
regularly considers other matters involving the Torrance Municipal Airport. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. 
A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable 
that the decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the official's interests. 
(Section 87103.) 
Section 87103 defines interests from which a conflict of interest may arise and includes 
among those interests: 
• Any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth $2,000 
or more. (Section 87103(b).) 
Based on the information you provided, Councilmember Griffiths has a real property 
interest in his home. (Section 87103(b).) 
Foreseeability: To give rise to a conflict of interest under the Act, the effects of decision on 
a public official's financial interests must be both foreseeable and material. (Section 87103.) 
Generally, a financial effect is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the interest is explicitly 
involved in the decision. Regulation 1870l(a) provides: 
"A financial effect on a financial interest is presumed to be reasonably 
foreseeable if the financial interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a 
governmental decision before the official or the official's agency. A financial 
interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision involves the issuance, 
renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement 
to, or contract with, the financial interest, and includes any governmental decision 


affecting a real property financial interest as described in Regulation 
I 8702.2(a)(I )-(6). 11 
File No. A-16-226 
Page No. 4 
None of the standards set forth above apply to your facts. If the real property interest is "not 
explicitly involved," as here, a financial effect is only reasonably foreseeable if the effect can be 
recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. (Regulation 
18701(b).) Based upon the facts provided, a financial effect is reasonably foreseeable because it is a 
realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. 
Materiality: Regulation 18702.2( a) uses 12 different standards (including the standards in 
Regulation 18702.2(a)(I) - (6) set forth above) to determine ifan effect on an interest in real 
property is material. Your facts indicate that the decision regarding the appeal of the Lexus 
Conditional Use Permit involves a specific parcel that is more than 500 feet from Councilmember 
Griffith's residence and the decision is not interrelated with other decisions involving the entire 
Airport parcel. 
Therefore, for purposes of analyzing this decision, Councilmember Griffiths may measure 
the distance from the boundary of his residence to the boundary of the affected parcel (as opposed 
to the boundary of the entire Airport). 
The factors applicable to your facts are: 
"( 10) Would change the character of the parcel of real property by 
substantially altering traffic levels or intensity of use, including parking, of 
property surrounding the official's real property parcel, the view, privacy, noise 
levels, or air quality, including odors, or any other factors that would affect the 
market value of the real property parcel in which the official has a financial 
interest; 
"(l 1) Would consider any decision affecting real property value located 
within 500 feet of the property line of the official's real property, other than 
commercial property containing a business entity where the materiality standards 
are analyzed under Regulation 18702.1. Notwithstanding this prohibition, the 
Commission may provide written advice allowing an official to participate under 
these circumstances if the Commission determines that there are sufficient facts to 
indicate that there will be no reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on the 
official's property; or 
"( 12) Would cause a reasonably prudent person, using due care and 
consideration under the circumstances, to believe that the governmental decision 
was of such a nature that its reasonably foreseeable effect would influence the 
market value of the official's property." 
Interrelated Decisions: Generally, decisions are analyzed independently to determine if 
there will be a foreseeable material financial effect on an official's financial interest. 


File No. A-16-226 
Page No. 5 
We have advised that under certain circumstances, some large, complex decisions may be 
divided into separate decisions so that even if an official has a disqualifying interest in one 
component of the series of decisions, he may still participate as to the other components in which he 
has no financial interest. (Milich Advice Letter, No. 1-04-216; Merkuloff Advice Letter, No. 1-90-
542; Huffaker Advice Letter, No. A-86-343.) Even if this can be accomplished, the Commission has 
advised that some decisions may be too interrelated to be considered separately. 
Where a decision in which an official has a conflict of interest is "inextricably interrelated" 
to another decision, the official will be required to disqualify from both decisions. Regulation 
18706(b) provides that "decisions are 'inextricably interrelated' when the result of one decision will 
effectively determine, affirm, nullify, or alter the result of another decision." 
Under the guidelines of Regulation 18706, the decision regarding the appeal of a Planning 
Commission decision to approve modification of South Bay Lexus' Conditional Use Permit for 
Parcel 30 is inextricably interrelated with both the decision to modify the lease by adding Parcels 46 
and 47 to the Lexus leasehold and the decision to move operations of the dealership on Parcel 52 to 
these added parcels. 
This is because a vote to grant or deny the appeal is essentially a vote for or against adding 
Parcels 46 and 47, and thus effectively determines or alters the result of the decision involving 
Parcel 52, the current site of the Lexus dealership showroom and sales offices. 
Therefore, if the Councilmember has a conflict in one of these decisions, he may not 
participate in any of the decisions. (Condrashoff Advice Letter, I-09-30.) 
View, Privacy, Noise, Odors or Air Quality: You stated that the Councilmember's residence 
is 3,300 feet from Parcel 30, the subject of the appeal before the city council, and even further from 
Parcels 46 and 47, which may be added to the Lexus leasehold depending on whether the appeal is 
granted. Based on the facts provided, and the analysis by the City's Community Development 
Department, the significant distance between the Councilmember's property and Parcels 30, 46 and 
47 suggests that the Councilmember's residence will not experience issues with view, privacy, 
noise levels, or air quality issues from these sites. 
Traffic, Intensity of Use and Market Value of Official's Property: An approval of an 
amendment to the South Bay Lexus lease would increase the intensity of use in Parcels 46 and 47, 
which are currently vacant lots. More intense use of these parcels may also alter traffic levels on 
Crenshaw Boulevard, which runs along the Eastern and Southeastern portion of the Airport parcel. 
However, Parcels 46 and 47 are approximately 3,300 feet away from the Councilmember's 
residence. Due to the distance, your facts suggest that the decision regarding an appeal of a 
Planning Commission decision to approve modification of South Bay Lexus' Conditional Use 
Permit would not affect the market value of the Councilmember's property on Cricklewood Street. 
Interrelated Decision: 
The Councilmember's residence is within 500 feet of Parcel 52, the current site of the Lexus 
dealership showroom and sales office, and this decision is inextricably interlinked with the other 
decisions discussed above. However, your facts suggest that even though this parcel is nearby, other 


File No. A-16-226 
Page No. 6 
established dealership properties, a freeway and city streets buffer the area between the properties, 
and thµs the Councilmember's residence will not experience significant issues with view, privacy, 
noise levels, or air quality issues from the site. Moreover, your facts suggest there will not be 
substantial alterations of traffic levels, intensity of use, parking, or factors that would affect the 
market value of the Councilmember's residence. 
Accordingly, the decisions will not have a material financial effect on Councilmember 
Griffith's real property interest. Therefore, he does not have a disqualifying conflict of interest in 
the decisions in question. 
ER:jgl 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel 
;ft24n-£-r<: 
By: 
Emelyn Rodriguez 
U 
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

Permit?

in question. However, if the facts should change regarding the appeal and related decisions you 
should seek additional advice.

None of the standards set forth above apply to your facts. If the real property interest is "not

interest;

within 500 feet of the property line of the official's real property, other than

are analyzed under Regulation 18702.1. Notwithstanding this prohibition, the

was of such a nature that its reasonably foreseeable effect would influence the

542; Huffaker Advice Letter, No. A-86-343.) Even if this can be accomplished, the Commission has 
advised that some decisions may be too interrelated to be considered separately.

to another decision, the official will be required to disqualify from both decisions. Regulation

and 47 to the Lexus leasehold and the decision to move operations of the dealership on Parcel 52 to
